reasoning:
The option-adjusted spread for the ICE BofA High Yield Emerging Markets Corporate Plus Index experienced a rapid, outlier spike in early April 2025, surging from around 3.84 on April 3 to a peak of 4.90 on April 9, before slightly settling at 4.77 on April 11 (the value carrying into April 13). Over the past year historically, this index had not exceeded 4.20. Market outlooks attribute this recent widening to a sudden bout of tariff-related recession fears, but they also emphasize that emerging market corporate fundamentals remain exceptionally strong (with expected default rates below 1%). Because this spike is driven largely by sentiment and liquidity rather than fundamental deterioration, the spread is highly likely to mean-revert over the coming month. 

For the very near-term date (April 20), volatility remains high and the spread is hovering right around the 4.77 threshold, though momentum slightly favors a decrease (P=0.43). Over the 1-month horizon (May 13), mean reversion should be the dominant force, pulling expected spreads closer to the ~4.20 mark and making it quite unlikely to remain above the extreme 4.77 level (P=0.26). For the longer-term horizons (July and October), the expected value of the spread continues to favor remaining below 4.77, but the forecast variance increases significantly. With more time, the probability of fresh macroeconomic shocks or sustained trade disruptions occurring and pushing spreads back to these elevated levels rises, thus pushing the probabilities back up toward maximum uncertainty (0.50).
